C:
Buy it — and for a B2B product specifically, this isn't close. Build your own only if auth itself is your product, or you have a hard requirement (air-gapped/on-prem, data residency, a regulatory regime) that no vendor satisfies.

Here's the reasoning.

**B2B auth is mostly the expensive, undifferentiated part.** Selling to businesses means you'll quickly need enterprise SSO (SAML and OIDC against Okta, Entra/Azure AD, Google, Ping), SCIM user provisioning/deprovisioning, organizations/multi-tenancy, role-based access, MFA, and audit logs. None of that makes your product better — it's table stakes that *gates deals*. The first time a prospect says "we can't buy unless you support our SAML IdP," you don't want that to be a quarter of engineering work. Vendors turn it into a config screen.

**The cost of building is the long tail, not the login box.** A username/password form is a weekend. The real bill is everything after: secure session handling, token rotation, password reset flows, breach monitoring, rate limiting, bot defense, and then *each enterprise IdP's particular interpretation of SAML* — which is where you'll lose weeks per customer. It's also a permanent on-call and security-liability surface. A single mistake here is a credential-leak headline, not a bug ticket.

**It's a one-way door that gets harder to reverse.** Auth sits at the center of your data model. Migrating off a homegrown system later (after you have real customers and sessions) is far more painful than wrapping a vendor from day one. Buying keeps the early decision cheap and reversible-ish.

What I'd actually do:

- **Own your identity data model** — the `users`, `organizations`, and `memberships` tables, and the mapping of external identity to your internal authorization. Keep authorization (what a user can *do* in your app) in your own code. This is the part that's specific to you, and it keeps you from being fully locked in.
- **Buy the protocol-heavy, security-heavy layer** — authentication, session issuance, SSO/SAML, SCIM, MFA.

On vendors (pick by stage, all current as of early 2026):

- **WorkOS** — purpose-built for exactly the "we need to sell to enterprise" path; SSO/SCIM/audit logs are the core product, and pricing stays free until you have enterprise connections. Strongest fit if B2B/enterprise is the explicit goal.
- **Clerk** — best developer experience and pre-built UI, with solid organizations/multi-tenancy support; good when you want to move fast and own less front-end.
- **Auth0 (Okta)** — most mature and flexible, but gets expensive and complex at scale; the safe incumbent choice.
- **Self-hosted (Keycloak, Ory, Zitadel)** — only if you need to run it yourself for compliance/on-prem reasons. This is "buy the software, host it yourself" — it removes vendor cost and data-residency concerns but brings back the operational burden, so it's a middle path, not true building.

The one case for genuinely building: auth/identity *is* your differentiator (you're selling an IdP, a fraud product, or something where the auth flow is the value), or you have a constraint that rules every vendor out. Outside that, buying is faster to revenue, more secure by default, and the thing that actually unblocks enterprise sales.

If you tell me your stage (pre-launch vs. has-customers), whether on-prem/data-residency is in play, and your stack, I'll narrow this to a single vendor pick.

**What would change this answer.** Flip me toward build/self-host if any of these is true: a compliance or sovereignty constraint (air-gapped, on-prem, strict data residency, FedRAMP-style regime) that no SaaS vendor can satisfy; auth/identity is itself your differentiator; or you're at a scale where vendor per-connection pricing (Auth0/WorkOS enterprise tiers) credibly exceeds the fully-loaded cost of a dedicated identity team — including on-call, security, and SAML edge-case maintenance. The honest limitation of this recommendation: it's a default for the common case, not a costed comparison for *your* numbers — the build-vs-buy crossover depends on your enterprise-customer count, deal sizes, and engineering cost, which I don't have yet. If you've already built solid auth and it's working, "rip it out and buy" is usually not worth it; this is mainly a guide for what to do going forward.

If you tell me your stage (pre-launch vs. has-customers), whether on-prem/data-residency is in play, and your stack, I'll narrow this to a single vendor pick.
P:
Buy it. For a B2B product this isn't close: build your own only if auth itself is the product you sell, or you have a hard constraint no vendor meets (air-gapped/on-prem, strict data residency, a regulatory regime that rules them all out). Everyone else buys.

Here's the reasoning.

**B2B auth is the expensive, undifferentiated part — and it gates deals.** Selling to businesses means you'll soon need enterprise SSO (SAML and OIDC against Okta, Entra ID, Google, Ping), SCIM provisioning and deprovisioning, organizations/multi-tenancy, role-based access, MFA, and audit logs. None of it makes your product better; it's table stakes that *unblocks revenue*. The first time a prospect says "we can't sign unless you support our SAML IdP," you don't want the answer to be a quarter of engineering work. A vendor turns that into a config screen and a sales-unblocking "yes."

**The cost of building is the long tail, not the login box.** A username/password form is a weekend. The bill comes after: secure session handling, token rotation, password-reset and recovery flows, breach and credential-stuffing defense, rate limiting, bot mitigation — and then *each enterprise IdP's particular reading of SAML*, which is where you lose a week per awkward customer. It's also a permanent on-call and security-liability surface. A mistake here isn't a bug ticket; it's a credential-leak headline.

**It's a one-way door that gets harder to reverse.** Auth sits at the center of your data model, and every user, session, and token deepens the dependency. Migrating off a homegrown system after you have real customers is far more painful than wrapping a vendor on day one, when the switching cost is near zero.

What I'd actually do — the split that matters:

- **Own your identity *data model*.** Keep the `users`, `organizations`, and `memberships` tables, and the mapping from external identity to your internal authorization, in your own database. Keep authorization — what a user is allowed to *do* in your app — in your own code. This is the part that's specific to you, and owning it is what keeps a vendor swap from being a rewrite.
- **Buy the protocol-heavy, security-heavy layer.** Authentication, session issuance, SSO/SAML, SCIM, and MFA. This is the commodity with the highest blast radius if you get it wrong.

**The honest catch with buying:** pricing climbs as you scale, and you take on some lock-in. The decision-relevant difference between vendors is the pricing *model*, not today's numbers. Per-enterprise-connection pricing (WorkOS) tracks how B2B actually grows — few users, but each SSO connection maps to a real, paying deal. Per-MAU pricing (Auth0, Clerk) is usually fine for B2B early because user counts are low, but watch the tier where enterprise SSO gets gated behind a much pricier plan. Confirm current pricing directly; all of these have changed it more than once.

Vendor shortlist (verify pricing before you commit):

- **WorkOS** — purpose-built for exactly the "we need to sell to enterprise" path. SSO, SCIM, and audit logs are the core product; priced per enterprise connection, with the base user-management tier free to a high MAU ceiling. Strongest fit when enterprise B2B is the explicit goal.
- **Clerk** — best developer experience and pre-built UI, with solid organizations/multi-tenancy. Pick it when shipping fast and owning less front-end matters more than maximum enterprise depth.
- **Auth0 (Okta)** — most mature and flexible, the safe incumbent. Gets expensive and complex at scale, and the enterprise features you need sit in the higher tiers.
- **Self-hosted (Keycloak, Ory, Zitadel)** — only if you must run it yourself for compliance, on-prem, or residency reasons. This is "buy the software, host it yourself": it removes vendor cost and residency concerns but brings back the operational and patching burden. A middle path, not true building.

The one real case for building from scratch: identity *is* your differentiator — you're selling an IdP, a fraud/auth product, or something where the login flow itself is the value — or a constraint rules every vendor out. Outside that, buying is faster to revenue, more secure by default, and the thing that actually unblocks enterprise sales.

Default path if enterprise B2B is the goal: own the data model, buy the protocol layer, and start with WorkOS — or Clerk if developer experience and pre-built UI outweigh enterprise depth. Two things would change the pick: if on-prem or data residency is in play, go self-hosted; if you're already post-launch with live sessions, the migration path needs a closer look. Tell me your stage and stack and I'll confirm the single choice.
